items.title: Household Economy
Story: I began my jurny at the age of 12 by creating a household economy. Each of my siblings attempted to start their own currencies, but they struggled to maintain them because they lacked trust in their own systems and frequently created new money. This eventually caused the value of their currencies to collapse. Through this experience I began learning the importance of consistency, structure, and trust in any economic system.

items.title: First Brand Item
Story: My first branded item was a bracelet made from recycled soda can pop tabs, finished with a slip-knot clasp. It was a simple project, but it marked the beginning of my interest in designing and creating physical products.

items.title: Museum
Story: After starting the household economy, my next project was building a small museum in my bedroom. I displayed World War II models, wooden dinosaur puzzles, and other creations I had made. Many of the items were suspended from the ceiling, and I used strings and curtains to reveal or hide them during presentations.

items.title: Evolution of the Brand Name
Story: Themadhood did not appear overnight. The name evolved through several iterations before becoming what it is today. It originally began with the username 'mpa', simply a three-letter combination used to satisfy minimum username requirements for games.

Over the span of roughly two months the name evolved through several stages: mpa ⇒ mpamap ⇒ nosidam ⇒ mad ⇒ MadHood ⇒ aMadHood ⇒ TheMadHood ⇒ Themadhood. Many of these changes happened because I frequently forgot passwords and had to create new accounts.

I eventually moved away from 'nosidam'. Although I liked the name, my mother suggested it might be misinterpreted and encouraged me to change it. I shortened it to 'mad', which led to jokes from gaming friends, so I expanded it into 'MadHood'. This was the first time I experimented with capitalization. The word 'Hood' was inspired by Robin Hood, a historical figure I was deeply interested in at the time and had studied through books, documentaries, and films.

When creating my email account, I discovered that 'MadHood' was already taken, so I briefly used 'aMadHood'. Soon after, I wanted something more distinctive and adopted 'TheMadHood'. Because many games used case-sensitive usernames, the name eventually shifted into a consistent single form: 'Themadhood', which became the final version.

This also led to the name 'Themadhood Pequot.' The surname 'Pequot' came from a combination of influences. I had learned through family genealogy that a distant relative that had Pequot ancestry, which connected the name to my family history. At the same time, the name itself sounded strong and distinctive, and it also resonated with the region of Pequot Lakes, Minnesota. Together, those factors made 'Pequot' feel like a natural fit. Since 'Themadhood' functioned as a first name for the persona, adding 'Pequot' completed the identity while also serving as a tribute to both family heritage and the place that influenced the name.

items.title: 2nd & 3rd Brand Items
Story: A mentor introduced me to basic wire-working techniques using paperclips. After learning the fundamentals, I expanded on those skills by studying jewelry techniques from books and experimenting with making clasps, links, and bead designs. From there I began producing bracelets and anklets as additional handmade products.

items.title: Business Cards
Story: I began creating business cards to share my contact information and brand. The earliest versions were simple printed pieces of paper. Over time they evolved into proper printed cards, and today I often prefer using branded pens as a practical and memorable alternative.

items.title: Portable Shop
Story: At age 15 I attended two homeschool groups. At the end of the Thursday group I set up a small portable shop near the door where I sold handmade trinkets and small crafts. At the Friday group I set up the same shop once per month.

items.title: Box Shop Upgrade
Story: After leaving the Thursday homeschool group I continued selling at the Friday group. I improved my portable shop by adding a sign and refining my product designs, including upgrading the pop-tab bracelet clasp from a slip knot to a buckle.

During this period I also experienced my first business lesson. I allowed someone to photograph one of my pop-tab bracelet designs without purchasing it. A few weeks later I saw similar designs sold as kits in major stores. Although their versions were priced higher, the experience taught me the importance of protecting designs and intellectual property.

items.title: YouTube
Story: I began uploading videos to YouTube and experimented with recording, editing, and publishing content. Although the technical process was straightforward, the videos received little engagement. After several weeks I decided to move on to other projects while keeping the channel available for future use.

items.title: Logo
Story: I designed my first logo, marking an early step toward building a recognizable brand identity.

items.title: Website
Story: After turning 18 I created several websites for my different brand projects. While they did not receive much traffic or generate many sales, they served as important learning experiences in web development and online publishing.

items.title: Covid Crash
Story: During the COVID-19 pandemic, many businesses shifted toward online ordering. Despite that trend, my small ventures experienced a significant decline in activity, going from occasional sales to almost none.

items.title: Themadhood's Goods on Etsy
Story: I opened an Etsy shop to sell products online. While the platform provided a simple way to list items, the $0.20 listing fee combined with low sales meant the shop was not profitable. For the time being I removed the listings until there is a better strategy for relaunching.

items.title: Creation Of Themadhood's Codes
Story: I began learning programming seriously in 2019 with the goal of building applications. In spring of 2023 I created a GitHub account and formally established Themadhood's Codes as the development branch of the project.

items.title: Themadhood's Codes Split
Story: As development projects grew more specialized, I began organizing them into smaller infrastructures and sub-brands under Themadhood's Codes. This structure made it easier to manage different tools and systems without needing to completely rebrand each project later.

items.title: Creation of Themadhood (Music, Photos, Videos)
Story: In spring I reconnected with a friend I had been searching for for nearly a decade. The experience inspired me to write a song, but I struggled to translate the melody into a finished piece.

Because of the growing availability of AI music tools, I experimented with several generators and eventually found one that allowed me to refine lyrics, experiment with melodies, and test the flow of the song by hearing it performed.

After completing the first song, I began revisiting older material and rewriting several songs into a full album. The tracks were scheduled to release sequentially on YouTube starting at midnight on New Year's Day 2026.

The project expanded quickly. I later released a New Year affirmations album in February and began preparing additional albums. As the creative work grew, Themadhood Media was reorganized into a parent branch overseeing Themadhood Music, Photos, and Videos.

items.title: Logo Clean UP
Story: I redesigned and cleaned up my logo after years of resizing and reuse had caused visual quality to degrade. The new version was rebuilt at a 2000 × 2000 resolution and saved as layered artwork so it can be resized cleanly in the future.

items.title: Emergency Website Restore
Story: Sometime between December 2025 and January 2026 my previous hosting provider shut down without warning. I only discovered the problem when a journalist from Slash Music attempted to contact me about covering my release of 'The Unreachable Flower'.

I quickly launched a temporary site on Wix so that basic information would remain accessible. However, because I manage many assets and layouts across multiple branches, rebuilding everything through Wix was slow.

I realized that writing a simple custom site would be faster. I used ChatGPT to analyze one of the Wix sites i had made and generate a base template. From there I rebuilt the pages, uploaded the assets, and restored the main sites within about a week.
I am now continuing to refine and restore the sites to their full functionality.
